questions.question: Explain the difference between seek time and rotational latency in disk access.
keyPoints: Seek time is the time to move the read/write head to the correct track/cylinder
Rotational latency is the time waiting for the desired sector to rotate under the head
Seek time typically ranges from 2-10 ms, rotational latency depends on RPM
Seek time is usually the larger component of disk access time

questions.question: What is disk caching? How does it improve system performance?
keyPoints: Disk caching uses portion of RAM to store frequently accessed disk data
Reduces access time by serving reads from fast memory instead of slow disk
Write-back cache delays writes to disk, improving write performance
Cache hit ratio determines effectiveness - frequently accessed data stays in cache

questions.question: Describe the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm with an example.
keyPoints: Also known as elevator algorithm - moves arm in one direction servicing all requests
When it reaches the end, it reverses direction and services remaining requests
Reduces starvation compared to SSTF (all requests eventually get serviced
Example: arm at cylinder 50, requests at 10, 80, 30, 100 - goes 50→80→100 then reverses to 30→10

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of SSDs over traditional hard disks?
keyPoints: Advantages: faster access time (no moving parts), lower latency, more durable, less power, silent operation
Disadvantages: higher cost per GB, limited write cycles, lower maximum storage capacity than HDD
SSDs use flash memory - electronic storage vs magnetic/mechanical in HDD
Best used for operating systems and frequently accessed data, HDD for mass storage

questions.question: Explain the concept of RAID Level 1 and RAID Level 5.
keyPoints: RAID 1 (mirroring): duplicates data on two or more disks, provides fault tolerance but uses 50% capacity
RAID 5 (distributed parity): stripes data with distributed parity, can recover from single disk failure
RAID 1+0 combines mirroring and striping for best performance and reliability
RAID improves performance through parallelism and reliability through redundancy

questions.question: What is disk formatting? Differentiate between low-level and high-level formatting.
keyPoints: Low-level formatting: creates physical sector structure (tracks, sectors, inter-sector gaps) - done at factory
High-level (logical) formatting: creates file system structure (boot sector, FAT/inode tables, root directory)
Partitioning creates logical divisions before high-level formatting
Formatting prepares blank disk for storing files with appropriate file system

questions.question: Calculate the average rotational latency for a disk rotating at 7200 RPM.
keyPoints: Formula: Average rotational latency = (1/2) × (60/RPM) seconds
Average rotational latency = 4.167 milliseconds
This is the average time to wait for the desired sector assuming it could be anywhere on the track

questions.question: Explain the disk access time formula. A disk has the following specifications: seek time = 5 ms, rotational speed = 10,000 RPM, transfer rate = 50 MB/s, and controller overhead = 1 ms. Calculate the time to read a 10 KB file assuming the required sector is already under the head.
keyPoints: Disk access time = seek time + rotational latency + transfer time + controller overhead
Rotational latency = 60/(2×10000) = 3 ms (average for 10000 RPM)
Transfer time = 10 KB / 50 MB/s = 10×1024 / (50×1024×1024) = 0.2 ms approximately
Total = 5 + 3 + 0.2 + 1 = 9.2 ms (if sector not under head, add rotational latency)
If sector is under head, skip rotational latency: 5 + 0.2 + 1 = 6.2 ms

questions.question: Compare FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, and C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithms. Illustrate with an example where the initial head position is at cylinder 50 and pending requests are at cylinders: 95, 180, 34, 119, 11, 45, 62, 24. Calculate total head movement for each algorithm.
keyPoints: SSTF gives minimum movement but may cause starvation; SCAN provides uniform wait time

questions.question: Describe the physical structure of a magnetic hard disk. Explain terms like platter, track, sector, cylinder, and head. Also discuss factors that affect disk performance.
keyPoints: Physical structure: one or more circular platters coated with magnetic material, mounted on a central spindle
Data organized in concentric circles called tracks, each track divided into sectors
Cylinder = all tracks at same radius across all surfaces - can be accessed without arm movement
Read/write heads float above surface on air cushion - one head per surface
Performance factors: seek time (arm movement), rotational latency (rotation wait), transfer rate
Other factors: controller overhead, queuing delay, disk cache hit ratio
Zone bit recording allows outer tracks to have more sectors, increasing capacity

questions.question: Explain RAID technology. Discuss different RAID levels (0, 1, 5, 0+1, 1+0) in terms of performance, fault tolerance, and minimum number of disks required.
keyPoints: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) combines multiple disks for performance and/or reliability
RAID 0: Striping only - no redundancy, minimum 2 disks, best performance but no fault tolerance
RAID 1: Mirroring - duplicates data, minimum 2 disks, 50% capacity, good fault tolerance
RAID 5: Distributed parity - minimum 3 disks, can recover from 1 disk failure, good read performance
RAID 0+1: Striping then mirroring - combines performance of RAID 0 and fault tolerance of RAID 1, minimum 4 disks
RAID 1+0: Mirroring then striping - more fault tolerant than 0+1, can survive multiple disk failures (if in different mirror sets)
Choice depends on requirements: performance vs reliability vs cost
